name: Binary Search for Bug-Introducing Commits with Bisect
description: Use git bisect to perform an efficient binary search through commit history and pinpoint the exact commit that introduced a bug.
trendingReason: As codebases grow, bisect is increasingly used in CI pipelines to auto-detect regressions across hundreds of commits.
prerequisites: A known good commit where the bug did not exist
A way to test whether the bug is present (manual or script)

steps.title: Start the bisect session
steps.description: Initialize bisect mode. Git will guide you through a binary search of commits between a known good and bad state.

steps.title: Mark the current commit as bad
steps.description: Tell Git that the current HEAD contains the bug. This sets the upper bound of the search range.
steps.tip: You can also specify a commit hash: 'git bisect bad <hash>' if HEAD is not the bad commit.

steps.title: Mark a known good commit
steps.description: Provide a tag, branch, or commit hash where the bug was not present. Git will now check out the midpoint between good and bad.
steps.tip: If unsure which commit was good, try a commit from a few weeks ago and narrow down from there.

steps.title: Test and mark each bisect step
steps.command:
# Run your test, then mark:
git bisect good
# or if the bug is present:
git bisect bad
steps.description: Git checks out a commit halfway between good and bad. Test it, then mark it. Git halves the range each time, finding the culprit in O(log n) steps.
steps.warning: If a commit cannot be tested (e.g., it does not build), use 'git bisect skip' to skip it. Git will try a nearby commit instead.

steps.title: Automate bisect with a test script
steps.description: Provide a command that exits 0 for good and non-zero for bad. Git will run it at each step automatically until it finds the first bad commit.
steps.tip: You can use any script: 'git bisect run ./test-bug.sh'. The script must be in the working tree or an absolute path.

steps.title: End the bisect session
steps.description: Return to the original HEAD and end bisect mode. Git will output the first bad commit hash before resetting.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# git-bisect-debug.sh — Automated bisect with a test command
set -euo pipefail

GOOD_REF="${1:?Usage: $0 <good-ref> <bad-ref> <test-command>}"
BAD_REF="${2:?Usage: $0 <good-ref> <bad-ref> <test-command>}"
TEST_CMD="${3:?Usage: $0 <good-ref> <bad-ref> <test-command>}"

echo "Starting bisect..."
echo "  Good: $GOOD_REF"
echo "  Bad:  $BAD_REF"
echo "  Test: $TEST_CMD"
echo ""

git bisect start
git bisect bad "$BAD_REF"
git bisect good "$GOOD_REF"

echo ""
echo "Running automated bisect with: $TEST_CMD"
echo "========================================="
git bisect run $TEST_CMD

echo ""
echo "=== Bisect result ==="
git bisect log | tail -5

echo ""
echo "Resetting to original HEAD..."
git bisect reset
echo "Done."

faq.question: How many steps does bisect take to find the bad commit?
faq.answer: Bisect uses binary search, so it takes approximately log2(n) steps where n is the number of commits in the range. For 1000 commits, that is roughly 10 steps.

faq.question: What if a commit in the range cannot be tested?
faq.answer: Use 'git bisect skip' to tell Git to try a different nearby commit. If too many commits are skipped, Git may not be able to pinpoint the exact first bad commit but will narrow the range.

faq.question: Can I bisect for a fix instead of a bug (find the first good commit)?
faq.answer: Yes. Swap the meanings: mark the commit where the fix is present as 'good' and where it is absent as 'bad'. Alternatively, use 'git bisect terms' to define custom terms like 'old' and 'new'.
